Johannes Paulus II, P.
P., 'Quid nos Vergilius doceat,' Latinitas 30
(1982) 3-6; 'Papal Oration Commemorating the Bimillennium,, Vergilius 28
(1982) 1-3.
1982-83
The present survey of Vergilian scholarship is the twenty-first
in the Vergilius series. Classical
World 68:1 (1974) published in association with The
Vergilian Society, Inc., contains my 'Recent Work on Vergil: A Bibliographical Survey, 1964-73'. The survey was reprinted in The Classical World
Bibliography of Vergil (Walter Donlan, ed.), Garland Publishing, Inc. (New
York & London 1978). Subsequent reviews have appeared in Vergilius 19
(1973), 20 (1974), 21 (1975), 22 (1976), 23 (1977), 24 (1978), 25 (1979),
26 (1980), 27 (1981), and 28 (1982).
The Bimillenary celebrations have given rise to a relentless stream of commentary, criticism, exposition and exhibition. Several journals
Gymnasium
90 (1983), Helmantica
33 (1982), Listy Filologické (Symposium Vergilianum: 16th International Eirene Conference) 106 (1983); Omnibus 3 (1982);
Würzburger Jahrbucher für die Altertumswissenschaft 8 (1982) have highlighted Vergilian material and the parade of exhibition catalogues provides a
marvelous perspective of Vergiliana in public and private collections.
The present compilation is indebted to the assistance and cooperation of
Mills Memorial Library of McMaster University, to the Robarts Library of the
University of Toronto, to the library of the Institute of Classical Studies and
Hellenic and Roman Societies, and to the Library of the University of London. Offprints of articles and bibliographic notices for inclusion in the annual
Vergilius review were received abundantly and scholars are once again asked
to send material to the editor of Vergilian Bibliography, c/o Department of
Classics, McMaster University, Hamilton, Ontario, Canada L8S 4M2.

raw_text:
Nutting's song for the Latin class was composed for the earlier Bimillennium. Oroz Reta offers details on Vergilian observances in Spain. Pasqualetti's enthusiasm is manifest in 24 alcaic stanzas. Polara provides a precis of
the commemorative lectures on Vergil's Eclogues in Naples (1981). Rome's
reporters interviewed M. C. J.Putnam after his commemorative lecture with
startling headlines .Uno studioso americano attaca il mito deVeroe troiano.
Putnam's contribution to the conference was entitled 'The Hesitation of
Aeneas.' Schilling gives a global view of the poet at each stage of his development. Roberto De Simone, in an impressive format, expands on the mediaeval
Vergil and the Neapolitan legends with a marvelous array of Grüninger woodcuts.

tidy perspective of present-day scholarship on many aspects of Vergil's opera

of the Atti of the 'Convegno mondiale' in Mantua, Rome, and Naples during

Simun Sonje, 'O prijevodu Vergilijere Eneide od Dure Hidze,' Živa
Antika 32 (1982) 185-93.
Batter offers a French verse translation of segments of Aeneid 4. Fisher
studies Vergil's Messianic Eclogue and Constantine's Oration to the Assembly
of Saints (177-82), and juxtalinear translations of Vergil and Cicero (183-9).
Kraggerud has published a rhythmic prose translation of Aeneid I in Norwegian. Seven volumes are forecast. Sonje's article examines a Yugoslavian translation.

Batter offers a French verse translation of segments of Aeneid 4. Fisher
studies Vergil's Messianic Eclogue and Constantine's Oration to the Assembly
of Saints (177-82), and juxtalinear translations of Vergil and Cicero (183-9).
Kraggerud has published a rhythmic prose translation of Aeneid I in Norwegian. Seven volumes are forecast. Sonje's article examines a Yugoslavian translation.

R. D. Williams (ed.), Virgil: The Eclogues and Georgics (London, New
York 1979). Rev: Bernard Frischer, CP 78 (1983) 77-81.
Geymonat favours Bucolica over Eclogae as the original title. Lyne's introduction (xiii-xxii) to the reprint of Day Lewis' translation of the Eclogues
and Georgics takes a fresh position on several questions, and provides generally useful explanatory notes (129-48). Roberts explores the arrangement of
the Eclogues and argues that it 'dramatizes a journey into the protected
pastoral preserve' followed by a return to the dangerous universe outside.
Todera^cu studies the vine, grape, pinecone etc., in the Bucolics for their
symbolic value.

Geymonat favours Bucolica over Eclogae as the original title. Lyne's introduction (xiii-xxii) to the reprint of Day Lewis' translation of the Eclogues
and Georgics takes a fresh position on several questions, and provides generally useful explanatory notes (129-48). Roberts explores the arrangement of
the Eclogues and argues that it 'dramatizes a journey into the protected
pastoral preserve' followed by a return to the dangerous universe outside.
Todera^cu studies the vine, grape, pinecone etc., in the Bucolics for their
symbolic value.

raw_text:
Stephen V. Tracy, 'Sepulcrum Bianoris: Virgil, Eclogues 9, 59-61,' CPh
77 (1982) 328-30.
Roberts interprets Eclogue 1 as Vergil's redefinition of poetry as a mode
of mimetic, not quasi-historical action, and states the limits
of linguistic
power. Mayer studies Eclogue 2 to determine Corydon's status and argues
that servile status
is unacceptable by reason of his property holdings and
poetic decorum.
Fisher settles on Aratus, who symbolized astronomy, as
solution to the question posed in Eel. 3.40. Conon and Aratus are suitable
partners on Damoetas' cups. Courtney suggests that Vergil projects himself
from the marriage of Antony and Octavia to the future and imagines the
sequel, the birth of a son, in Eclogue 4. His reading of Bue. 4.60-61 is: 'begin,
little boy, to recognize your mother with a smile (a supernatural thing to do);
ten months (because you are a boy, not a girl) have brought long weariness to
your mother.' Pulbrook's allegorical interpretation favours Daphnis as Octavian. Ogawa finds a principle of unity in the labor motif, a factor derived from
Theocritus Idyll
7.
Geymonat
revives Savage's earlier thesis (1963) that
Thyrsis is a reflection of the youthful Horace in Eclogue 7. Tracy's article
coincides with Frederick Brenk's study of the sepulcrum Bianoris (Vergilian
Bibliography, 1981-82, #26).

John Warden (ed),
Orpheus: The Metamorphosis of a Myth (Toronto
1982). Rev: M. Owen Lee, Phoenix 36 (1982) 380-82.
Hering's discussion of Georg. 1.1-42 offers useful asides. Kettemann detects a new, less pessimistic perspective at the close of Georgies 3. Primmer is
concerned with Georgics 3.40ff. and the design of the epic. Thomas attempts
to recover the full significance of asilus (G. 3.147) and traces the literary
history of the gadfly. His critical commentary, with a synthesis of technical
and literary aspects,
is masterly. Kraggerud's study of the Proteus passage
sheds fresh light on its relevance. Warden's collection contains W. S. Anderson's essay on the Orpheus theme. Vergil's Aristaeus, in Anderson's view, is a
young Aeneas, already compromised in a world of responsibility; Orpheus is
possessed by a cosmic furor, like Dido and Turnus.

raw_text:
Gordon Williams, Technique and Ideas in the Aeneid (London, New
Haven 1983).
Basson analyses the speeches of Jupiter (Aen. 1.257-96) and Anchises
(Aen. 6.752-892) along with the shield description (Aen. 8.626-731) to determine Vergil's view of Roman history. Effe examines VergiPs narrative technique and stresses emotional subjectivity in the epic. Lyne dismisses 'failure'
on the part of Aeneas or Vergil. Vergil never obscures the reality of war;
imperialistic
aggression
involves violence and
imperfect
motivations may
direct even the greatest hero. Phillips studies landscape in the epic and the
Georgics and traces progression in its poetic use from the Eclogues. Pöschl's
address underscores the humanism of Vergil's poetry. Scanlan provides topics
and questions based primarily on Aeneid 1-6 for secondary school teachers.
The questions are designed to excite discussion and serve as essay topics and
material for test papers.
Williams sheds new light on the deeper sense of classical poetry by providing an analysis of Vergil's literary technique; his search for meaning offers
new insights for thoughtful readers.
94.

Borghini, 'Un genetivo di inerenza, sunt lacrimae rerum' MD 4

raw_text:
Viktor Poeschi, 'Der Zweikampf zwischen Aeneas und Turnus/ Pietas,
Festschrift für Bernhard Koetting (E. von Dassmann & K. S. Franck, edd.)
JbACErg. Bd. VIII (Münster 1980) 349-55.
Codoñer comments on Aen. 1.81-123. Phillips suggests that Dido's behaviour at Aen. 1.561 does not reflect embarrassment or undue modesty; her
attitude is physically and psychologically apt. Borzsák detects oriental influence in the prodigy at Aen. 2.682ff. Cressey argues that Aen. 3.714 should be
assigned to Anchises' episode and not to Aeneas' last agony (pace R. D.
Williams, ad. loc.). Nenci stresses that humilemque (Aen.
3.522) denotes
'lowlying,' 'barely visible above the water surface." Quint underscores the
struggle between present and past in Aeneid 3 and examines episodes where
present actions are guided by memories of the dead past and where the theme
of forgetting appears with political implications for the new order of Augustus. Roti's view is that Aeneas stands 'alone' as sole leader after Anchises. Zijl,
Rix,
Pauw and Zeitsman unite in their comments on Aen. 3.568-87, and
655-81. Ameye analyses Aen. 4.298-9 (fama, eadem impia) and rejects the
notion of Aeneas' character as impius.
Bustamante treats Aen. 4.554-72.
Hardie accepts deliberate ambiguity in Aen. 4.48 If (and Aen. 6.796f) with a
conflation of images (mythological and scientific) of Atlas. Capponi treats
Aen.
5.213-217.
Rose examines
the
simile
at Aen.
5.270-81
as a foreshadowing of the loss of ships, separation from comrades, and safe arrivals in
Italy. Barigazzi studies the demons (Aen. 6.273-81) in conjunction with Cicero, De Nat. Deor, 3.17.44 (18.47) and Lucretius DRN 3.59-81. Kinsey
responds to D. P. Fowler (LCM 7: 5 [May 1982] 76) and excites Fowler's
renewed attack on Aen. 6.392-4; Kinsey's parting shot (?) responds to Fowler
again. The editor, J. Pinsent, grapples with the Gordian knot and suggests:
'You are armed and alive, so I cannot take you. I got no joy of previous cases
(also armed) and they were of divine descent' (as you are not, since Aeneas'
divine descent is unmentioned until 403-4). Pinsent prefers Fowler over Kinsey in the latest exchange. Wlosok studies Aeneid 6 with attention to the
function of Anchises' speech (724ff). Basto argues that Aen. 7.10 should be
seen in a symbolical and literal way; symbolically raduntur implies that the
Odyssey-half of the epic has ended; the adventures of Odysseus-Aeneas are
past and another invocation is required (Aen. 7.44-5). Blänsdorf introduces
Apollonius Rhodius, Argon.
2 and 3, into a discussion of Aeneid 7. Fowler
detects a possible acrostic in the long anacoluthic sentence at Aen. 7.601-4,

attitude is physically and psychologically apt. Borzsák detects oriental influence in the prodigy at Aen. 2.682ff. Cressey argues that Aen. 3.714 should be
assigned to Anchises' episode and not to Aeneas' last agony (pace R. D.
Williams, ad. loc.). Nenci stresses that humilemque (Aen.
3.522) denotes
'lowlying,' 'barely visible above the water surface." Quint underscores the
struggle between present and past in Aeneid 3 and examines episodes where
present actions are guided by memories of the dead past and where the theme
of forgetting appears with political implications for the new order of Augustus. Roti's view is that Aeneas stands 'alone' as sole leader after Anchises. Zijl,
Rix,
Pauw and Zeitsman unite in their comments on Aen. 3.568-87, and
655-81. Ameye analyses Aen. 4.298-9 (fama, eadem impia) and rejects the
notion of Aeneas' character as impius.
Bustamante treats Aen. 4.554-72.

responds to D. P. Fowler (LCM 7: 5 [May 1982] 76) and excites Fowler's
renewed attack on Aen. 6.392-4; Kinsey's parting shot (?) responds to Fowler
again. The editor, J. Pinsent, grapples with the Gordian knot and suggests:
'You are armed and alive, so I cannot take you. I got no joy of previous cases
(also armed) and they were of divine descent' (as you are not, since Aeneas'
divine descent is unmentioned until 403-4). Pinsent prefers Fowler over Kinsey in the latest exchange. Wlosok studies Aeneid 6 with attention to the
function of Anchises' speech (724ff). Basto argues that Aen. 7.10 should be

past and another invocation is required (Aen. 7.44-5). Blänsdorf introduces

Antonie Wlosok, 'Der Held als Ärgernis: Vergils Aeneas,' Universität
im Rathaus (Universität Mainz) Band II (Mainz 1983) 32-55; WJDA 8 (1982)
9-20.
Eubanks studies the importance of Achates' role as sympathetic complement to Aeneas' character. Feeney examines Aeneas' response to Dido's impassioned, eloquent speech (Aen. 4.305-30). Aeneas' hard-won self control
will not permit him to answer in the same tenor. He fails to respond effectively, but whose words would be effective against her tirade? Foley's collection includes Christine G. Perkell's essay "On Creusa, Dido, and. the Quality
of Victory in Virgil's Aeneid.' Wlosok responds to contemporary criticism of
64
describing the custom of opening the Gates of War. The initial letters, MARS,
are consonant with the subject-matter of the lines. Huxley draws on Aen,
3.392 and Aen. 8.84 to compose a witty quatrain. Senfter is concerned with
the morning-star simile attached to Pallas {Aen. 8.589-91). Basson grapples
with the order of names, the possibility of invention (and likely source) in the
names, and the rationale for their appearance as allies. He translates ara {Aen.
10.655) as 'land, border, frontiers.'
Saylor treats imagines in Aeneid 10 as
frustrating opponents of the heroes in the four main encounters of the battle
narrative. Venini understands Aen. 10.541 in the sense: superstans immolât
ingentique umbra tegens. McKay traces similarities between the Aristaeus
epyllion and
the
Camilliad
by examining
theme,
structural
details,
and
symmetrical arrangement in both tales. Farron responds to anti-Roman, antiAugustan aspects of the epic (#136) and explains the abrupt ending of the
epic as partly an attack on Rome and on the Augustan settlement. Elsewhere
(138) she examines English and Classical villains including among the latter
Sallusťs Catiline, Horace's Cleopatra, and Vergil's Mezentius. Vazquez aligns
the Vergilian simile at Aen. 12.710ff. with its prototype in Georg. 3.220-3.
Lyne finds parallels for the blush of Lavinia (Aen. 12.64-70) which are clearly
erotic, implying Lavinia's love for Turnus. An appendix lists a range of comments on Lavinia and on the simile.

raw_text:
John F. Hall, 'P. Vergilius Maro; Vates Etruscus,' Vergilius 28 (1982)
44-50.
154.
P.
Scuderi, 'Il mito eneico
in età augustea:
aspetti filoetruschi e
filoellenici,' Aevum 52 (1978) 88-99.
Castagnoli provides a tidy conspectus of the Aeneas legend in Latium in
association with recent archaeological discoveries. Hall's article studies Phocas' 5th century title of Vates Etruscus for Vergil. He examines the Etruscan
background of Mantua, Vergil's cognomen, the Etruscan origins of the Aeneas
myth, and Etruscan claims of descent from Trojan forefathers. Hall argues
that Vergil constructed his Trojan-Etruscan alliance to reflect Etruscan support for Augustus, and to provide mythological precedent for an Italy where
Trojans (Romans) and Etruscans
work together to ensure a well-ordered
society.

Basson
is concerned with Graviscae (Aen. 10.184) and the sources of
malaria (endemic, a Lydian import, or contagion spread by Hannibal's troops
from North Africa). Dubourdieu treats the Pratica di Mare finds since 1950.
The thirteen altars,
marking the federal sanctuary
of Venus (?), and the
Heroon of Aeneas together signalize the shift in emphasis from the legend of
the Trojan arrival in Latium to the Trojan origins of Rome. Horsfall, with a
tribute to A. M. Guillemin's L'Originalité de Virgile (Paris 1931) as uneven
but stimulating, explores Vergil's lack of interest in technical minutiae and in
65
the 'unheroic hero.'

title: Hall, 'P. Vergilius Maro; Vates Etruscus,' Vergilius 28 (1982) 44-50. 154. P. Scuderi, 'Il mito eneico in età augustea: aspetti filoetruschi e filoellenici,' Aevum 52 (
publication_block: 1978) 88-99. Castagnoli provides a tidy conspectus of the Aeneas legend in Latium in association with recent archaeological discoveries. Hall's article studies Phocas' 5th century title of Vates Etruscus for Vergil. He examines the Etruscan background of Mantua, Vergil's cognomen, the Etruscan origins of the Aeneas myth, and Etruscan claims of descent from Trojan forefathers. Hall argues that Vergil constructed his Trojan-Etruscan alliance to reflect Etruscan support for Augustus, and to provide mythological precedent for an Italy where Trojans (Romans) and Etruscans work together to ensure a well-ordered society

66
complete consistency; narrative economy and pace are paramount, especially
in his geographical allusions, and the poet is prepared to telescope or ignore
distances in favour of the momentum of the epic. The action of Aeneid 7-12
is a major instance of compression; three or four campaigning seasons of the
annalistic tradition transpire in a matter of days ...
To provide a coherent
synthesis for Aeneid 6,
in Horsfall's view,
is misguided. Homeric, Pythagorean-Orphic and
Stoic
theories
have
an
independent
existence.
Byrsa,
Dido's citadel, derives from an Akkadian word meaning 'stronghold' according to Huss, but was confused with another Punic word suggesting 'ox-hide'
(cf. Aen. 1.365-8). Noll's illustrated article treats hunting scenes from the
Aeneas-Dido story on Coptic materials. Pane's picture book, a treasure-trove
of color and black-and-white photographs of the Phlegraean Fields, is an
attractive compendium. The special volume of Parola del Passato treats recent
excavations in Latium: at Castel di Decima, Lanuvium, Lavinium, and at sites
in Rome (Sanť Omobono, and Regia). Small's study of Cacus and Marsyas
has a bearing on Aeneid 8. Van den Bruwaene weighs the etymologies of
place names, people and goddesses in legends relating to Rome. The Albans,
Aeneas and the white sow, and the Sabines are included in the review.

Georgiche di Virgilio,' Prometheus 8 (1982) 97-116.

raw_text:
Antonie Wlosok, 'Vergil als Theologe: Iuppiter-pater omnipotens,'
Gymnasium 90 (1983) 187-202.
Basson examines the concept of peace in connection with the Golden Age
in Vergil's works. Coleman argues that the gods of the Hellenized literaryreligious tradition were not capable of bearing the serious burden of causation
that Vergil placed upon them. Den Adel counters Fontenrose's (1978) statement that 'there was no oracle' at Delos. Vergil, and most Greek and Roman
authors did believe in some prophecy there, although there are no extant
records of oracular procedures and no quotable utterances. Certainly Vergil
credits Delos with mantic activity (Aen. 3.92-6). Grimal's provocative article
examines Epicurean traits in the Georgies. Schilling's lecture provides a global
view of Vergil's work, commending his mastery of form and the serious tenor
of his philosophical thought.

raw_text:
Antonie Wlosok, 'Vergil als Theologe: Iuppiter-pater omnipotens,'
Gymnasium 90 (1983) 187-202.
Basson examines the concept of peace in connection with the Golden Age
in Vergil's works. Coleman argues that the gods of the Hellenized literaryreligious tradition were not capable of bearing the serious burden of causation
that Vergil placed upon them. Den Adel counters Fontenrose's (1978) statement that 'there was no oracle' at Delos. Vergil, and most Greek and Roman
authors did believe in some prophecy there, although there are no extant
records of oracular procedures and no quotable utterances. Certainly Vergil
credits Delos with mantic activity (Aen. 3.92-6). Grimal's provocative article
examines Epicurean traits in the Georgies. Schilling's lecture provides a global
view of Vergil's work, commending his mastery of form and the serious tenor
of his philosophical thought.

raw_text:
David West, 'pauca meo Gallo,' LCM 8: 6 (1983) 92-3.
Allison finds resonances of Vergil's treatment of Dido, of the structure of
the Aeneid, and of primary themes {furor, fides,and regnum) in Propertius 4,
7, and 8. Ball's survey of Tibullus contains material relevant to Vergil. Basto's
study of Horace, Odes 1.3 emphasizes the mock-heroic, Vergilian traits of the
propempticon. The parallels are evident largely in Aeneid 1-7. If sailing is
accepted
as a metaphor
for poetic
composition,
Horace
shares
Vergil's
audacia in poetry (with Prometheus, Daedalus, and Hercules). Collectively the
elements combine to suggest that there was some mutual influence between
the poets. Moles argues that Aen. 2.557-8 was influenced by Asinius Pollio's
account of and reflections on the death of Pompey the Great. Nicoli finds
recollections of the Aeneid structure in Ovid's account of Cupid, Apollo, and
Daphne.
Parsons treats the
Gallus
papyrus
recovered from Qasr Ibrim.
Romano detects further evidence of the encounter of Octavian with Vergil at
Atella in Horace, Odes 3.4.37-40. Vergil and Horace together read the Georgies to Octavian at Atella in 29 BC. Traill adopts a new approach to the
question of the relevance of Vergil to the second half of Horace, Odes 1.3.
Horace progresses from the condemnation of the first mariner, through the
gigantomachy, to the contemporary political scene. Vergil's departure for
Greece, judging by the closing stanzas of the ode, may have been politically
motivated. The poet may have been an agent for Augustus or Maecenas
. . . West re-examines the Gallan fragment from Qasr Ibrim.

raw_text:
David West, 'pauca meo Gallo,' LCM 8: 6 (1983) 92-3.
Allison finds resonances of Vergil's treatment of Dido, of the structure of
the Aeneid, and of primary themes {furor, fides,and regnum) in Propertius 4,
7, and 8. Ball's survey of Tibullus contains material relevant to Vergil. Basto's
study of Horace, Odes 1.3 emphasizes the mock-heroic, Vergilian traits of the
propempticon. The parallels are evident largely in Aeneid 1-7. If sailing is
accepted
as a metaphor
for poetic
composition,
Horace
shares
Vergil's
audacia in poetry (with Prometheus, Daedalus, and Hercules). Collectively the
elements combine to suggest that there was some mutual influence between
the poets. Moles argues that Aen. 2.557-8 was influenced by Asinius Pollio's
account of and reflections on the death of Pompey the Great. Nicoli finds
recollections of the Aeneid structure in Ovid's account of Cupid, Apollo, and
Daphne.
Parsons treats the
Gallus
papyrus
recovered from Qasr Ibrim.
Romano detects further evidence of the encounter of Octavian with Vergil at
Atella in Horace, Odes 3.4.37-40. Vergil and Horace together read the Georgies to Octavian at Atella in 29 BC. Traill adopts a new approach to the
question of the relevance of Vergil to the second half of Horace, Odes 1.3.
Horace progresses from the condemnation of the first mariner, through the
gigantomachy, to the contemporary political scene. Vergil's departure for
Greece, judging by the closing stanzas of the ode, may have been politically
motivated. The poet may have been an agent for Augustus or Maecenas
. . . West re-examines the Gallan fragment from Qasr Ibrim.

Gordon Williams, Figures of Thought in Roman Poetry (London &
New Haven 1980). Rev: W. S. Anderson, CO 59 (1981-2) 91-2.
Block's article, which treats the apostrophe to Nisus and Euryalus (Aen.
9.446ff.) in greatest detail, shows that Vergil, through a narrator who articulates an idiosyncratic response, sought to emphasize the distinct perspective
of the audience. Cleary studies the use of captured arms. Turnus' use of a
spolium as a weapon of war marks his impiety when compared to Aeneas.
Spolia are properly dedicated to Mars or Romulus; sometimes they are destroyed, and on other occasions they are exhibited in homes or temples as
signs of past victories. Görler's study of syntax, a theme treated in his Bimillenary Lecture at the Villa Vergiliana (July, 1982) is a thorough treatment of
Vergilian style. Hinojo treats Vergil's use of the poetic adjective in the Eclogues and Georgics. Moskalew's study is a major contribution to the study of
verbal repetitions,
their function and effect. The study is extremely well
documented, extraordinarily useful, and informative. Nagle's treatment of
Aen. 2.801-4 explores the means by which Vergil produces a strong sense of
'closure' and yet maintains forces for continuation. Rieks, in a valuable article, treats Vergilian style and language along with Homeric parallels. Smith's
review and clarification of the areas of phonetic controversy is accomplished

raw_text:
Edward L. Harrison, lAeneid 9.240,' Vergilius 28 (1982) 62.
Green responds to Cressey (LCM 6: 5 [May 1981] ) who interpreted A en.
1.455 (operumque laborem) as 'the work of navvies'. Green's article advances
Ausonius, Mosella, 305-7, where operumque labores is used and hardly admits
Cressey's interpretation. Green suggests a variant reading for A en. 'A55-artificumque manus interne (Mynors, inter se) operumque laborem. Interne suggests suppressed wonder; inter se, or intra se, suggests something more emotional. Harrison offers a new reading of Aen. 9.240, si fortuna permittitis usi
(Mynors, uti), and translates "if, exploiting fortune, you send us through to
seek Aeneas and the walls of Pallanteum ..."
226.
A.
Barchiesi, 'Le molte voci di Omero.
Intertestualità e
trasformazione del modello epico nel decimo dell'Eneide,' MD 4 (1980) 9-58.

Barchiesi studies Homeric background to the deaths of Pallas and Lausus,
the tears of Hercules, and the belt of Pallas; Vergil characteristically highlights
deeper, psychological and humane implications. Harrison cites Homer, Iliad
5.311-453 as background to Vergil's Venus; there are echoes at the start
(Ascanius-Cupid episode) and at the end (Venus' magical cure). Vaughn supports Servius' dictum that Vergil alludes not only to words and ideas but also
to the structure of Theocritus' Idyll-Book. Van Sickle challenges the thesis
and the argument. West provides a synoptic study of Vergil, Aen. 1.42-5 and
70
through a metrical analysis of all enclitic rhythms in the poetry of Vergil.
Smith's painstaking study sheds considerable light on a troublesome question
in Latin phonology, the accentuation of enclitic composites.

raw_text:
Edward L. Harrison, lAeneid 9.240,' Vergilius 28 (1982) 62.
Green responds to Cressey (LCM 6: 5 [May 1981] ) who interpreted A en.
1.455 (operumque laborem) as 'the work of navvies'. Green's article advances
Ausonius, Mosella, 305-7, where operumque labores is used and hardly admits
Cressey's interpretation. Green suggests a variant reading for A en. 'A55-artificumque manus interne (Mynors, inter se) operumque laborem. Interne suggests suppressed wonder; inter se, or intra se, suggests something more emotional. Harrison offers a new reading of Aen. 9.240, si fortuna permittitis usi
(Mynors, uti), and translates "if, exploiting fortune, you send us through to
seek Aeneas and the walls of Pallanteum ..."

M. Vietti, 'Pathos virgiliano in Macrobio,' AAT 113 (1979) 219-43.
André's article provides details on Vergilian influence in Seneca. Vidal
studies Vergilian
traits
in Petronius and in Seneca's Letters. Rychlewska's
article extricates allusions to the Eclogues in Nonius Marcellus (3rd century
AD) and makes a useful contribution to textual criticism of the Bucolics.

Luigi
Lehnus'
article
in
Scripta
Philologa
(179-211) addresses the biographical notices attributed to M. Valerius Probus,
the first century grammarian. He provides a new text of Probus' account of
Vergil's life with divergences from Egnazio's 1507 Venetian edition (which
located Vergil's birthplace three miles from Mantua). Naumann deals with
fact and fiction in the Vergilian Lives.. Sanz Ramos is concerned with Vergilian Parthenius, the traditional scripsit Aeneidem . . . sed non emendavit; and
admonente Pollione scripsit Bucolicam. Vidal offers a critical analysis of the
Phocas
Life and provides a translation. Von Albrecht, with characteristic
clarity and suggestion, deals broadly with Vergil's life and work, his sources
and technique. Wünsche treats a probable portrait of Vergil in Munich's
Glyptotek.

raw_text:
Th. Ziolkowski, 'Broch's Image of Vergil and its Context,' Modern
Austrian Literature 13/14 (1980) 1-30.
Abbolito's article treats Vergilian influences on Avitus' biblical epic. André
detects survivals of Vergil
in the works of Delacroix, Berlioz, and SainteBeuve, and explores Vergilian traits
in Italian romanticism. Baldwin comments on Procopius of Gaza's use of Aen. 2.573 (communis Erinys) to describe the burden of taxation in his Panegyricus in Imp. Anast. 14. Certainly
Vergil was a familiar author in early Byzantium. Cattareo salutes Gavin Douglas' innovative use of the heroic couplet in his Aeneid translation. Fichter
argues that Ariosto, Tasso and Spenser read Vergil with the eyes of St. Augustine. All translated the implicit melancholy and uncertainty of the Vergilian
vision into a sure knowledge that the triumph of their chosen dynasties was
an image of the Christian apocalypse. De Teves Costa studies Vergilian influence in painted ceramic tiles of the 16th-18th century in Portugal's mansions
and the University of Evora. Forte examines traces of Vergil's Aeneid in Luca
Signorelli, the Florentine Apollonio di Giovanni, Raphael, Mephaeus Vegius'
Thirteenth Book (1428), Ariosto's Orlando Furioso (1532), and Tasso's Gerusalemme Liberata (1581). Fuhrmann traces the influence of Vergil's Georgics
in the European tradition. Gransden treats W. H. Auden's jeu d'esprit, 'Secondary Epic' included in his Homage to Clio (1960). For Auden, Vergil's was 'a
Muse betrayed' by political consideration. Huxley offers a puzzle based on
five Vergilian verses. Kallendorf's doctoral thesis treats 14th and 15th century
criticism of Vergil, primarily Cristoforo Landino's Vergil Commentary (1488)
75

De Teves Costa studies Vergilian influence in painted ceramic tiles of the 16th-18th century in Portugal's mansions and the University of Evora. Forte examines traces of Vergil's Aeneid in Luca Signorelli, the Florentine Apollonio di Giovanni, Raphael, Mephaeus Vegius' Thirteenth Book (1428), Ariosto's Orlando Furioso (1532), and Tasso's Gerusalemme Liberata (1581). Fuhrmann traces the influence of Vergil's Georgics in the European tradition. Gransden treats W. H. Auden's jeu d'esprit, 'Secondary Epic' included in his Homage to Clio (
publication_block: 1960). For Auden, Vergil's was 'a Muse betrayed' by political consideration. Huxley offers a puzzle based on five Vergilian verses. Kallendorf's doctoral thesis treats 14th and 15th century criticism of Vergil, primarily Cristoforo Landino's Vergil Commentary (1488) 75

in Italian romanticism. Baldwin comments on Procopius of Gaza's use of Aen. 2.573 (communis Erinys) to describe the burden of taxation in his Panegyricus in Imp. Anast. 14. Certainly
Vergil was a familiar author in early Byzantium. Cattareo salutes Gavin Douglas' innovative use of the heroic couplet in his Aeneid translation. Fichter
argues that Ariosto, Tasso and Spenser read Vergil with the eyes of St. Augustine. All translated the implicit melancholy and uncertainty of the Vergilian
vision into a sure knowledge that the triumph of their chosen dynasties was

and the University of Evora. Forte examines traces of Vergil's Aeneid in Luca

Thirteenth Book (1428), Ariosto's Orlando Furioso (1532), and Tasso's Gerusalemme Liberata (1581). Fuhrmann traces the influence of Vergil's Georgics
in the European tradition. Gransden treats W. H. Auden's jeu d'esprit, 'Secondary Epic' included in his Homage to Clio (1960). For Auden, Vergil's was 'a
Muse betrayed' by political consideration. Huxley offers a puzzle based on
five Vergilian verses. Kallendorf's doctoral thesis treats 14th and 15th century

where Vergil emerges as Magister Morum. McKay examines Vergilian traits in
paintings by Claude Lorrain, Nicolas Poussin, Peter Brueghel, and A. J.M.
Turner. Martin records his impression of a poetic palimpsest of the Aeneid
composed by Denis Guenon. The contemporary version, which introduces
analogies with the Boat People, draws on the first six books to yield three
distinct episodes. The modern poem is a free adaptation with a powerful
impact. Mueller is concerned with the interaction of epic and dramatic traditions in Racine's use of the Dido tragedy as a model for Bérénice (230-248).
Michael Murrin's study contains fourteen pages on Vergil. Nascimento's article treats Nicholas Trevet, an English Dominican at Oxford during the 13th
and 14th centuries, who composed the earliest known commentary on Livy.
His commentary on the Bucolics survives in two 15th century North Italian
mss., one in the Laurentian Library, Florence, the other in Evora (Portugal).
Pope's doctoral thesis argues that Spenser's use of national history in his
heroic poem was directly modelled on Vergil's use of historical material in the
Aeneid.
She supplies new interpretations of the function of prophecies in
Aeneid 1-6 and explores Aeneas' reasons for his liaison with Dido. Rudat is
concerned with fatum, pietas, sapientia, and labor in the Aeneid as reflected
in the great themes of Chaucer's work. Schuchard deals with Nahum Tate's
Dido and Aeneas (1689) and includes illustrations by Franz Cleyn. Sinclair is
concerned with the Aeneid as sacrum poema in Macrobius Sat. 1.24.13. The
temple metaphor at
Sat. 1.24.12 leads to the conclusion that the sacrum
poema (= Aeneid) is not to be regarded as pagan counterpart to the Bible.
Siro treats an anonymous work in the Laurentian Library, Florence, drawn
from late antique or early mediaeval times. Goldsmith's doctoral thesis is
concerned with a prose translation of the Aeneid by Joost van den Vondel,
enhanced with 140 pen and ink drawings by Leonaert Bramer. Thill's article
traces Vergilian elements in Jacob Balde's 17th century Latin and German
odes
(Carmina Lyrica). Wofford's doctoral thesis argues that Vergil's technique dramatizes the limitations and bleakness of the world wherein his characters act.
